sentences.text: That's correct.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.

So after about eight days-- I don't remember exactly-- eight days or nine days, we arrived in Rowne, which is R-O-W-N-E, a place, a town, in Eastern Poland, in Wolyn, where my cousin of my father, Witek Stok or Witold Stok, had a factory similar to the factory of my grandfather in Warsaw, a soap factory.
And he was well-known locally because he was the local capitalist, so to say.
And they lived in a nice villa, in a very nice house in the middle of quite large fruit orchard, garden.
Otherwise, Rowne, I remember, was an Eastern European town.
Everything was, of course, much smaller.
The houses were single-story or two-story.
And it was all together, of course, much more behind, so to say, in comparison to Warsaw.
When we came, we moved into the-- we moved into the villa of Witek.
Witek and his wife, they had no children and the house was quite big, so they accommodated all of us.
I think all nine of us, yes, in the beginning, at least.
I'm not sure what happened later.
And then immediately on the day when we came or maybe one day later, there was an absolutely horrific bombardment, by the Germans, which was one of the worst.

Luckily they drug trenches in the garden and we were hiding in those trenches, although they were not properly protected, but one heard bombs falling nearby and the noise of the-- of the anti-aircraft machine guns.
And there was-- and then there was a general pandemonium and people were screaming and shouting.
It was a really terrible experience.
And after maybe 40 minutes, when we got out of those trenches there, I remember that the whole street was opposite nearby, everything was burning.
Lots of houses were burning.
And we were so frightened by that bombardment as well that we thought of leaving Rowne all together.
But a few days later, two or three days later, the Red Army came.
And that was part of the [INAUDIBLE] of Poland between the Germans and the Russians that was a result, a direct result of the Molotov-Ribbentrop pact signed in Moscow in August 1939.
And that whole part of Poland where we were, the whole eastern part of Poland from the frontier with Soviet Union to the River Bug, which was now to the west of us, was occupied by the Red Army.
When the Red Army came, we saw them in the street, coming in the street.
And that was one of those memories that I still carry with me until today.
They were not-- they were-- because it was an amazing view.
They didn't come in one in a column, but they came rather in groups, in bands.
They had-- they looked rather disorganized.
They had still their old type of pointed helmets, pointed heads like, which I later found out were used by them during the revolution.
And they all together looked like some kind of partisan bands then a proper army.
And of course, we were glad in one way that the Russians came, because that meant that there were no more bombardments.

On the other hand, a day or two later, after the Red Army came, some military with-- with blue and red, blue and red insignia on-- on their hats, with a I think blue ring around their hat, came to my-- to the villa of my-- of my father's cousin and asked for him to come to the military command.
Now, he wasn't there at that moment, so they left a paper with his wife and asked that he should appear there next day.
And when she asked if he has to take anything, like warm clothes or something, they said, no.
No.
It's just a short-- we'll have a brief interview and that's going to be over in an hour's time or maybe a bit less.
And he will come back.
There is absolutely nothing to worry.
Which means the Russian secret police.
And they arrested him because-- they arrested him, obviously, because he was the local factory owner.
And because of that, he was an hostile element.
And he was later sent to Siberia.
He was in a gulag, in a labor camp.
And he died there or was killed during the war.
But we didn't find it out except late-- except after the war.
And his wife, of course, never saw him again.
When he didn't reappear, of course we were-- immediately we understood that something is wrong.
And since my father had the same name, Stok, and they-- when the [INAUDIBLE] came, asked for Mr.
Stok, so it was lucky that my father was not there, because they would have arrested him instead probably.
So we thought it would be-- we can't stay any more in the villa and who moved out of the villa, and rented for a while a room elsewhere in Rowne.

And then we moved out of Rowne all together and we started wandering for weeks in eastern Poland, in Wolyn, moved by peasants' carts, usually, or going-- going with a peasant cart and a horse somewhere in very muddy roads.
It was raining, because it was already late September or October.
And we moved from one place to another.
I'm not quite sure where to.
And the one place that I remember, the one memory that I have is that we went to-- we came to a place called Miedzyrecz, which was a-- a little town, a shtetl.
And that must have been Yom Kippur or-- or the evening of Yom Kippur.
And I went with my father to the synagogue.
And that synagogue was, of course, a very, very traditional synagogue.
And I remember still those figures of praying people covered in their tallis and the yellow candles.
It-- it was really something which I never saw before, so it was, of course, a very vivid memory.
And then we came back to Rowne, I think, for a short time and still stayed for some days in Rowne.
And we were after that wandering.
All together, the eastern Poland was-- it was a real wandering of the nations.
People were moving from one place to another, because nobody knew where it is better, where it is safer.
There were absolutely incredible masses of refugees who had lost their houses and lost their possessions and they were going from one place to another.
And we lost track, as well, of-- of-- of the family that we came to Rowne, my-- the sister of my father and with her family.
She moved later to Wilno.
And we finally, after another some weeks, we came to Bialystok, which is another town in northeastern Poland.
And the family or part of the family of my mother was there.
How they got there and what they were exactly doing there, I'm not sure.
But there was my mother and my-- and my parents and a couple of aunts, couple of her sisters, and my grandmother, my mother's mother, and some other family members.
And we lived all in one quite big and darkish apartment, where one room was as well two Russian women were living.
Those Russian women, I was told-- I think they were-- they were as well [INAUDIBLE] women.
And of course, the Russians would-- would confiscate a flat or a part of a flat to put their own people.
And so we were living with those Russians in one flat.
And as for-- and in eastern Poland in those days, the situation was totally unclear.
It was not obvious what was going to happen.
And the Russian authorities demanded of people that they declare themselves whether they want to retain the Polish citizenship or whether they want to remain the Soviet citizens.
And it was pretty-- whether they want to became-- to become Soviet citizens.
And it was pretty clear that there were consequences.
But nobody knew exactly what consequences were going to be.
And my parents obviously didn't want to agree to become Soviet citizens, because as far as we were concerned, it could have been forever.
But what happened was that those people who declared that they want to become-- that they want to accept Soviet nationality were left more or less in peace.
Those, however, who didn't want to, who said that they want to keep their Polish nationality, were later on deported in great numbers to Siberia.
They were-- they basically prepared for them transports, cattle trucks.
And they took them in great numbers to Siberia.
And of course, nobody knew how it was going to end ever.
And of course, the fear of Siberia was probably bigger than of anything else.

So quite a number of people, although they must have realized that that was not a very good way of-- of continuing, decided all the same to go back to Warsaw or wherever they came from in western Poland, although it was occupied by Hitler.
That, of course, was not at all permitted.
There was absolutely no way that one could go legally from eastern Poland to the western one.
The only way to do it was in crossing the border illegally.
There was a border on the River Bug and you could only cross it illegally.
And if they caught you on-- on this occasion, then the consequences were very serious.

If the Russians would catch you, then it was certain that they would lock you up in prison and certainly deport you to Siberia or maybe keep you in prison or send you to even more-- more awful labor camp.
In any case, that was something consequences which were almost unthinkable.
All the same, my family members, one after one, they decided that they want to go back to Warsaw.
Well, the-- we didn't know.
We didn't know.
No.
We didn't know exactly.
But I think Russians were at this stage more feared than the Germans.
And in fact, the Germans did catch us.
But that's-- we are coming to that.
But-- so members of my family, they just went one by one and went back to Warsaw.
And my mother, who was very keen on her beautiful apartment in Warsaw and the things that she left and the furniture and so on, it was all left behind.
And as far as we knew, it could all have been completely destroyed or stolen or something.
So she decided to go back to Warsaw.
And her sister went back to Warsaw.
So they disappeared.
And other family members went to Warsaw.
And finally, there were in this flat only one of my aunts, who was a painter, one of the sisters of my mother, and my father and myself and my grandmother and maybe one other aunt.
I'm not quite sure.
Now, in Bialystok, I heard on the whole-- I-- I think I quite enjoyed it.
I learned quite a bit of Russian.
I was going to Russian-- to Russian [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] official ceremonies that are in the theater.
I remember that was kind of a theater spectacle for the children, I think, where the whole row of young pioneers appeared in the-- in their white shirts and red scarves.
And they were singing.

And people were aware, of course, that things will not stay as they were, because the one song that I learned then, and the one song that the pioneers were-- were singing, which I still remember, is [POLISH], if tomorrow the war breaks out, If tomorrow the breaks out, if tomorrow we have to march, if the dark force-- force is on the border, and so on, then we will all stand together to defend the countrymen, something like that.
And I learned all kinds of Russian poems.
I was on the way to become quite-- quite a integrated Soviet citizen.
But it didn't happen, because my father decided finally as well to cross over to Warsaw.
As my mother went already, he-- it was more or less obvious that we will try as well to go to Warsaw.
So we went with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father, my cousin Jurek, who was then a young boy, a teenager-- he was maybe 17 or 18, something like that-- and myself.
And-- How long did you stay in Bialystok altogether?
Altogether, about six months, I think, because that was in early April.
That was in--
That was like in '40.
Oh, it was '40, yes.
Yes.
Because--
--of course, the--
The beginning of the war was September--
So then we wandered around.
And then we came to Bialystok probably in November.
And we stayed there through the winter, '39/'40.
And that was probably in April 1940.
And how it was organized exactly that we were-- that we managed to go to cross over to Warsaw I'm not quite sure.
But there were-- there was a whole organized chain of people who must have been paid and who helped us to cross that border.
We-- and it was, of course, we couldn't use any official means of transport.
Anyway, it was not so far to the border, because Warsaw to Bialystok is maybe 150 kilometers, so to the border it must have been, oh, maybe a bit more.
But maybe it was 80 kilometers, something like that.
So we were traveling on peasants'-- on peasants' carts and basically peasants' clothing or more or less-- well, everybody was dressed in remnants of what they had of their old clothing.
And whenever it came to a little village or a town where there was a danger that we would--
--come across the police or soldiers, we would then walk all around and walk in the fields to avoid any-- any town.
I think we passed in one place where there was a market.
And we slept in peasants' houses.
We slept in pretty isolated peasants' houses in-- and those peasants' houses, I still remember some of them.
And finally, after two or three days, we came near to the border.
And there, a whole group of-- of people were formed with a guide, or even two guides.
And we had to walk that night.
And that night we crossed the border.
It took a long time.
It was-- but first of all, the walk was very long.
It was about 30 kilometers all together.
And we had to walk in-- in a file, in a-- in an Indian file.
And we had to stop.
And whenever there was-- we were coming near the border post or-- or there was a danger of a patrol, we would need to fall down and lie down and not make a sound.
And it was still-- there was snow around still, although it was already April.
And it took a long, long time.
And we were lying down for quite often and waiting for-- until the guide gave the sing that it was possible to continue.
And doing that, we came to the barbed wire.
There was barbed wire and big fencing of barbed wire.
And the-- they lifted somehow the bottom wires and crawled under.
And children, that's me and another child, I think, were thrown over the barbed wire.
And this way we crossed it.
And then we found ourselves in the German part of Poland.
And I think-- and next years I remember well.
And we were-- again, we walked for the remainder of the night and finally came to the peasants' house.
And in this peasants' house, after I think a pretty short time, suddenly a Polish police appeared.
They must have found us.
Either somebody told them.
They were-- probably somebody told them.
And it started with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- with personal searches.
And they searched for money or-- or valuables and searched everybody.
But I had in fact on me a kind of sheepskin fur jacket and there were some things sewn in in this, like a diamond or a couple of diamonds, and they didn't find it.
That was-- maybe they didn't search me so, so, so--
--so closely.
But they didn't find it.
And then the police took us out of there.
And-- but what happened was that it was-- it was spring and there were floods all over.
Bug must have--
--must have run out of banks.
And there were those enormous amounts of water everywhere.
There was-- it was really amazing.
There was-- it was as if the whole of the countryside was completely flooded.
And we had to wait.
And finally, they found somehow a boat.
And they went with us in a boat and final-- and then they-- I think they gave-- they handed us over on the other side somehow to the German gendarmerie.
But the German gendarmerie, whether they were busy with other things or whether they were not interested, they just kept us for an hour or so and they then let us go.
So we were suddenly left free and still in this flooded area.
And we slept somewhere even in a peasants' house again in the flooded area.
It was--
I don't know exactly what it was called.
But it was on the River Bug, which is the--
Which is now, I swear, a border, an eastern border of Poland.
Now it is the border between Poland and Ukraine, or Poland and Belarussia.
And then it was border between the Soviet Union and the German Generalgouvernement, what was the part of Poland occupied by Germans, what was called Generalgouvernement.
Right.
So we went to-- so after that, after we were freed, we went back to Warsaw.
And we found ourselves in Warsaw in April, 1940.
And in Warsaw, we went back-- we moved back into our apartment.
The apartment was not exactly what it was before.
Because for the start, we were not the only people living in the apartment.

But there was as well a family of my mother, some family which we didn't know-- which I didn't know before the war, her uncle and cousins and-- and cousins and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some other family members.
And they lived-- they moved-- they had to leave Lodzh.
I'm not quite sure-- which is Lodz.
I'm not quite sure if they had to leave or if they left Lodz out of their own will.
Anyway, they found themselves in Warsaw.
And indeed, they spend the rest of the war in Warsaw.
And they were in our apartment as well.
So that was one thing.
End of Tape 2.
Goodness gracious.
